papers.title: Identification of young nearby runaway stars based on Gaia data and the lithium test
papers.author_names: R. Bischoff M. Mugrauer G. Torres T. Heyne O. Lux V. Munz R. Neuhäuser S. Hoffmann A. Trepanovski

abstract.text: Young nearby runaway stars are suitable to search for their place of origin and possibly associated objects, for example neutron stars. Tetzlaff, Neuh\\\\"auser & Hohle (2011) selected young ($\\\\le 50$ Myr) runaway star candidates from Hipparcos, for which they had estimated the ages from the location in the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram and evolutionary models. Here, we redetermine or constrain their young ages more precisely not only by using the new Gaia DR2 data, but also by measuring lithium, which is a youth indicator. For 308 stars, we took spectra to search for the strong resonance doublet of the lithium-7 isotope at 6708 $\\\\unicode{xC5}$. The spectra were taken with the \\\\'Echelle spectrograph FLECHAS at the University Observatory Jena between February 2015 and June 2018 and with TRES between April 2011 and June 2017 at the Fred L. Whipple Observatory. We found 208 stars with significant occurrence of lithium in their spectra, and five possess a possible age younger or about 50 Myr. Three of these targets are even closer than GJ 182, the nearest known runaway star at about 24 pc. Theses stars are young runaway stars suitable for further investigation of their origin from either a dynamical or supernova ejection.

brief.tldr: [research paper] A spectroscopic survey of 308 young runaway star candidates from Tetzlaff et al. (2011) using Gaia DR2 data and lithium absorption line measurements at 6708 Å reveals 208 stars with detectable lithium, confirming youth; five show ages ≤50 Myr, with three closer than GJ 182 (24 pc), suggesting dynamical or supernova ejection origins. Lithium detection provides independent age constraints beyond Hertzsprung-Russell diagram modeling, enabling more precise identification of young runaway stars for origin tracing.

source_fields.title: Identification of young nearby runaway stars based on Gaia data and the lithium test

source_fields.sections_text: Footnotes: The section lists footnotes citing observational data and online resources from the University Observatory Jena, Fred L. Whipple Observatory, and the Rochester University stellar database. Discussion: The study identified 13 young dwarf stars in the sample with ages between 10 and 120 Myr, as determined by HRD and lithium depletion analyses, with key candidates like HIP 60831 and HIP 544 showing ages of ~50–175 Myr and strong lithium lines, suggesting potential young runaway star candidates. Introduction: This paper presents a novel spectroscopic investigation of young runaway stars using Gaia DR2 data and lithium absorption line measurements to confirm their youth and constrain ages, addressing the challenge of identifying and characterizing runaway stars from core-collapse supernovae in binary systems that are otherwise difficult to trace due to Galactic dynamics. | Age Estimation: The ages of the dwarf stars are estimated from their positions in the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram using isochrones from Bressan et al. (2012) with solar metallicity, and metallicities are found to have a negligible effect on age estimation due to their small impact compared to observational uncertainties. | Target Characterisation With Gaia Dr2 Data: This section details the characterization of 308 stars using Gaia DR2 data, identifying most as giants and excluding them from consideration as young runaway stars, while a small subset of dwarf candidates are highlighted based on their position in the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram and comparison with known dwarf properties. | Sample Selection, Observations And Data Reduction: A sample of 460 bright, observable stars from Tetzlaff et al. (2011) was selected for high-SNR spectroscopic observation using the FLECHAS spectrograph at the University Observatory Jena, with additional radial velocity data obtained for 30 objects via the TRES spectrograph. | Li (6708 Å) Equivalent Width Measurements And Abundances: This section describes the method and results of measuring lithium (6708 Å) equivalent widths in stellar spectra, using a Ca (6718 Å) line for wavelength calibration and determining Li abundances based on spectral analysis and classification.

abstract: Young nearby runaway stars are suitable to search for their place of origin and possibly associated objects, for example neutron stars.
For 308 stars, we took spectra to search for the strong resonance doublet of the lithium-7 isotope at 6708 $\\\\unicode{xC5}$.
We found 208 stars with significant occurrence of lithium in their spectra, and five possess a possible age younger or about 50 Myr.
Theses stars are young runaway stars suitable for further investigation of their origin from either a dynamical or supernova ejection.
title: Identification of young nearby runaway stars based on Gaia data and the lithium test

catalog_assessment.evidence: Spectroscopic survey of 308 runaway star candidates from Tetzlaff et al. (2011) with new Gaia DR2 data and lithium measurements. Paper presents observational data for actual stars.

papers.title: Dippers from the TESS Full-Frame Images I: The Results of the first 1 year data and Discovery of A Runaway dipper
papers.author_names: Tomoyuki Tajiri Hajime Kawahara Masataka Aizawa Michiko S. Fujii Kohei Hattori Yui Kasagi Takayuki Kotani Kento Masuda Munetake Momose Takayuki Muto Ryou Ohsawa Satoshi Takita

abstract.text: We present a comprehensive catalog of the dippers---young stellar objects that exhibit episodic dimming---derived from the one year's worth of data of Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite ($ TESS$) full-frame images. In the survey, we found 35 dippers using the convolutional neural network, most of them newly discovered. Although these dippers are widely distributed over the first half-hemisphere that $TESS$ surveyed, we identified the majority's membership with the nearest association Scorpius--Centaurus, Velorum OB2, and nearby Orion molecular cloud complex. However, several dippers are likely to be located in the field. We also found three old dippers whose age exceeds ten million year, which is considered as the disk dissipation time. The color-color diagram indicates that these old dippers are likely to have an extreme debris disk. In particular, we found a runaway old dipper having a large three-dimensional velocity of $72 \\\\mathrm{km\\\\ s}^{-1}$. The dippers in the field, which were probably escaping from their birth molecular clouds or were born outside the current area of star forming regions, are more common than previously considered.

source_fields.sections_text: 6. Discussion: The experiment identifies seven unidentified dippers, including three old debris dipper systems with ages >10 Myr showing IR excess from debris disks rather than primordial disks, indicating a significant population of isolated young stars and YSOs outside known star-forming regions that remain undetected in current catalogs. 1. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel classification of "debris dippers"—episodic dimming in old, non-star-forming region stars like HD 240779—demonstrating that such dimming can occur beyond star-forming regions and challenges the assumption that dippers are exclusively linked to young, primordial circumstellar disks. 2. Identification of Dippers: This section describes the use of aperture photometry on TESS full-frame images to generate light curves and a convolutional neural network trained on eclipsing binaries to identify potential dipper candidates with episodic, deep photometric dips. 5. Runaway old dipper: TIC 43488669: TIC 43488669 is a runaway old dipper with a debris disk, aged 100–400 Myr (possibly older due to low metallicity), exhibiting deep, asymmetric dips likely caused by asteroids or debris in its dusty tail. Appendix A Reduced Unit Weighted Error: The Reduced Unit Weighted Error (RUWE) metric is used to identify single stars from binaries or unresolved systems in Gaia data, with high RUWE values indicating potential binary or multiple-star companions. 4. Characterization of individual stars: This section identifies and characterizes individual dippers in star-forming regions such as Sco OB2 and Chamaeleon using Gaia kinematic data and follow-up spectroscopy to determine their memberships and ages. 3. Disk and stellar properties of dippers: This section reports the discovery of 35 dippers with infrared excess, diverse dip characteristics, and detailed stellar and disk properties, showing a mix of membership and disk types primarily in Orion, TW Hydrae, and OB associations.

abstract: In particular, we found a runaway old dipper having a large three-dimensional velocity of $72 \\\\mathrm{km\\\\ s}^{-1}$.
title: Dippers from the TESS Full-Frame Images I: The Results of the first 1 year data and Discovery of A Runaway

catalog_assessment.evidence: Comprehensive catalog of 35 dippers from TESS data, including discovery of a runaway dipper with large 3D velocity. Paper presents object-level observational data with specific source detections.

papers.title: Runaway OB Stars in the Small Magellanic Cloud: Dynamical Versus Supernova Ejections
papers.author_names: J. Dorigo Jones M. S. Oey K. Paggeot N. Castro M. Moe

abstract.text: Runaway OB stars are ejected from their parent clusters via two mechanisms, both involving multiple stars: the dynamical ejection scenario (DES) and the binary supernova scenario (BSS). We constrain the relative contributions from these two ejection mechanisms in the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) using data for 304 field OB stars from the spatially complete, Runaways and Isolated O-Type Star Spectroscopic Survey of the SMC (RIOTS4). We obtain stellar masses and projected rotational velocities $v_r\\\\sin i $ for the sample using RIOTS4 spectra, and use transverse velocities $v_{\\\\rm loc}$ from $\\\\it{Gaia}$ DR2 proper motions. Kinematic analyses of the masses, $v_r\\\\sin i $, non-compact binaries, high-mass X-ray binaries, and Oe/Be stars largely support predictions for the statistical properties of the DES and BSS populations. We find that dynamical ejections dominate over supernova ejections by a factor of $\\\\sim 2-3$ in the SMC, and our results suggest a high frequency of DES runaways and binary ejections. Objects seen as BSS runaways also include two-step ejections of binaries that are reaccelerated by SN kicks. We find that two-step runaways likely dominate the BSS runaway population. Our results further imply that any contribution from $\\\\it{in-situ}$ field OB star formation is small. Finally, our data strongly support the post-mass-transfer model for the origin of classical Oe/Be stars, providing a simple explanation for the bimodality in the $v_r\\\\sin i $ distribution and high, near-critical, Oe/Be rotation velocities. The close correspondence of Oe/Be stars with BSS predictions implies that the emission-line disks are long-lived.

source_fields.sections_text: Summary: This study estimates that dynamical ejection and binary supernova mechanisms contribute approximately 80:20 to the population of massive runaway stars in the Small Magellanic Cloud, with dynamical ejections dominating based on kinematic, mass, and binary properties. Discussion: The SMC OB runaways are predominantly produced by dynamical ejections (70–80%) with a 30:70 runaway-to-walkaway ratio, consistent with models, and BSS ejections contribute less (runaway frequency ~0.020, walkaway frequency ~0.11), with total frequencies aligning between theoretical predictions and observational estimates. Introduction: This paper leverages Gaia DR2 kinematic data to statistically distinguish between dynamical ejection and binary supernova mechanisms for runaway OB stars, addressing the long-standing uncertainty in the origin of massive field stars by providing the first large-scale, data-driven assessment of these ejection pathways. OB Runaway Kinematics: This section analyzes the kinematics of 304 field OB stars in the SMC, finding that 65% have transverse velocities exceeding 30 km s⁻¹, indicating a significant runaway population likely formed through dynamical ejections, while accounting for selection biases and the presence of walkaways. Stellar Mass Analysis: Stellar masses of the RIOTS4 runaway OB stars are estimated using updated evolutionary models and spectral data, with careful consideration of uncertainties due to spectral classification, particularly for Be stars and binaries, to evaluate the relative contributions of dynamical and supernova ejection mechanisms. Stellar Rotation Analysis: The stellar rotation analysis reveals a bimodal distribution of $v_{r}\\sin i$ in the SMC OB stars, with a distinct population of fast-rotating Be/Oe stars and a majority of slowly rotating non-Be stars, consistent with predictions from low metallicity and enhanced rotational velocities.
